title: Parallel Orchestration Patterns for Shadow-Clone Lanes
summary: Survey of 5 parallel execution patterns evaluated for shadow-clone lanes with max 3 specialists per lane.

patterns.name: Scatter-Gather
patterns.description: Coordinator scatters work to multiple workers in parallel, waits for all responses, then aggregates results into consolidated output.
patterns.fit_rationale: Perfect match for 3-specialist lanes. Coordinator assigns independent sub-tasks to each specialist, waits for completion, merges results. Natural barrier synchronization.
patterns.use_when: Task is decomposable into independent chunks that can be processed simultaneously and combined.
patterns.example: Research task split into 3 source domains; each specialist investigates one domain; results merged into unified finding.
patterns.failure_handling: Can proceed with partial results or retry individual workers

patterns.name: Pipeline (Stages)
patterns.description: Work flows through sequential stages; each stage can have parallel workers. Output of stage N feeds stage N+1.
patterns.structure: Stage1 → Stage2 → Stage3 (each stage may have parallel workers)
patterns.fit_rationale: Works well when task has natural phases (e.g., gather → analyze → synthesize). Each specialist can own a stage, or 3 specialists can parallelize within a stage.
patterns.use_when: Work has sequential dependencies but throughput matters; or different specialists have different capabilities suited to different stages.
patterns.example: Specialist 1 collects raw data, Specialist 2 validates/cleans, Specialist 3 produces final output.
patterns.coordination_overhead: Medium - requires stage handoffs and buffering
patterns.failure_handling: Stage failure blocks downstream; need retry or bypass logic

patterns.name: Map-Reduce
patterns.description: Map phase: split input and apply transformation in parallel. Reduce phase: aggregate mapped outputs into final result.
patterns.structure: Split → Map(parallel) → Shuffle → Reduce(parallel or serial)
patterns.fit_rationale: With 3 specialists, can assign 3 mappers then converge to 1 reducer, or have 2 mappers + 1 reducer. Best for homogeneous tasks.
patterns.use_when: Large input can be partitioned; same operation applies to each partition; results can be combined associatively.
patterns.example: Analyze 3 codebases independently (map), then synthesize common patterns (reduce).
patterns.coordination_overhead: Medium - requires shuffle/sort step between phases
patterns.failure_handling: Failed mappers can be retried independently; reducer failure requires re-reduce

patterns.name: Competing Consumers
patterns.description: Multiple workers pull from a shared work queue; each task processed by exactly one worker. Load-balances automatically.
patterns.fit_rationale: Good for variable-length tasks where you want automatic load balancing. Less control over which specialist gets which work. Works if specialists are interchangeable.
patterns.use_when: Tasks are independent and homogeneous; want automatic load distribution; don't need to control assignment.
patterns.example: Process queue of code review items; any available specialist picks next item.
patterns.failure_handling: Failed task returns to queue for another consumer; need idempotency or dedup

patterns.name: Fork-Join (Recursive Decomposition)
patterns.description: Recursively split task into sub-tasks, fork parallel execution, join results. Can nest arbitrarily deep.
patterns.fit_rationale: With only 3 specialists, deep recursion wastes the pattern's power. Best for 1-2 levels of forking. May over-complicate simple parallel work.
patterns.use_when: Task has natural recursive structure; sub-problems are independent; depth is bounded.
patterns.example: Break complex analysis into sub-analyses, each potentially splitting further, then join up the tree.
patterns.coordination_overhead: High - requires tracking fork/join tree and synchronization at each level
patterns.failure_handling: Complex - must propagate failures up the tree and decide on partial results vs full retry

recommendation.rationale: For shadow-clone lanes with max 3 specialists, Scatter-Gather offers the best balance of simplicity, control, and parallelism. The coordinator naturally maps to the lane orchestrator, and 3 workers fits the specialist cap exactly. Barrier sync at gather point ensures coherent results.
recommendation.secondary_rationale: Use Pipeline when specialists have differentiated capabilities or when work has natural sequential phases. Can combine: scatter-gather within a pipeline stage.
recommendation.avoid: Fork-Join is over-engineered for 3 specialists; Competing Consumers loses assignment control which may matter for specialist differentiation.

sources: AWS Prescriptive Guidance: Parallelization and Scatter-Gather Patterns for Agentic AI
Distributed Application Architecture Patterns - Scalability Patterns
